segments.title: Deterrence changes the force-structure question
segments.summary: A question about the cost of war turns into a force-structure point about deterring conflict before it starts.
segments.body: The answer argues that warfighting and deterrence are not the same planning problem, even though a navy needs both. A force optimized only to win after hostilities begin may not provide the posture, presence, or uncertainty that prevents an opponent from starting them. Dr. Clarke points out that deterrence rarely earns visible credit, which can make governments underweight the ships and readiness that keep a crisis below the threshold of war. The practical lesson is to test force structure against both missions instead of assuming combat power automatically supplies deterrence in the required form.

evidence.note: The question quotes a line about war being costly and Dr. Clarke contrasts warfighting needs with deterrence needs.
He says deterrence can imply a slightly different force structure, but navies usually need both.

segments.question: How does the line that war is costly compare with today's conflicts and the stream topic?
segments.answerShort: War is costlier than deterrence, so navies should ask what force posture deters conflict as well as what force can fight one.

segments.title: A Baltic bastion would defend convoys differently
segments.summary: A Baltic bastion answer contrasts Atlantic anti-submarine defense with local convoy air and missile defense.
segments.body: The answer separates two uses of the same strategic word. An Atlantic bastion protects ocean movement against submarines, while a Baltic version would help keep local sea lanes open under air, drone, and missile pressure. In that confined theater, local escorts would provide volume air defense while land-based fighters and heavier systems supplied the upper layer. The example points readers toward Polish Type 31-style air-defense frigates and Swedish convoy-cover concepts rather than a copy of Cold War Atlantic ASW.

evidence.note: The question asks whether a Baltic bastion similar to the Atlantic bastion would be useful.
Dr. Clarke says the Baltic case would focus on local command of the sea, convoys, air defense, drones, and missiles.

segments.question: Would it be a good idea to have a Baltic bastion similar to the Atlantic bastion?
segments.answerShort: Yes, but it would be a different concept: local convoy defense against air, drone, and missile attack rather than an Atlantic-style ASW bastion.

segments.title: Hypersonic missile drones still need targeting and range
segments.summary: A question about hypersonic missile drones gets a layered answer about command, targeting, range, and air defense.
segments.body: The answer pushes back against the idea that a new missile carrier automatically makes conventional navies helpless. Dr. Clarke emphasizes command and control, target finding, range limits, airborne early warning, electronic warfare, and defensive missiles. A weapon can be extremely fast yet short-ranged, forcing its uncrewed carrier close enough to be attacked by helicopters or other fleet assets. The useful point is that hypersonic weapons alter the threat picture, but they still sit inside a larger scouting, communications, and fleet-defense problem.

evidence.note: The viewer asks how threatened conventional navies are by naval drones carrying hypersonic missiles.
The answer stresses command and control, finding targets, air defense, airborne early warning, and electronic warfare.
Dr. Clarke notes that a very fast short-range missile may require the drone to enter helicopter and rocket-pod range.

segments.question: How badly do hypersonic missile drones threaten conventional navies?
segments.answerShort: They are a serious threat, but not magic; targeting, control, range, air defense, electronic warfare, and the carrier platform all matter.

segments.title: Interwar shipyard closures removed rearmament capacity
segments.summary: The panel explains how selling, demolishing, and legally restricting British shipyards after the Depression weakened later rearmament.
segments.body: The discussion distinguishes a dry dock that exists on paper from industrial capacity that is maintained and ready to use. It describes British authorities buying distressed shipyards and armament works after the Great Depression, then allowing machinery to be scrapped, sites to be built over, and covenants to prohibit renewed shipbuilding. When rearmament accelerated in the late 1930s, capacity could not simply be recreated on demand. This is a useful case study in why naval force structure depends on preserved yards, tooling, and skilled industrial infrastructure.

evidence.note: The panel says a paper dry dock is not usable capacity unless it is maintained.
It describes shipyards, machinery, and dry docks being sold, demolished, or restricted by covenant after the Depression.
The panel connects those closures to lost rearmament capacity in the late 1930s.

segments.title: A time traveller's list of naval decisions and lost evidence
segments.summary: A Doctor Who prompt identifies design choices worth changing and battles whose lost evidence historians would most value.
segments.body: The answers range from changing battlecruiser and Queen Elizabeth-class machinery decisions to preserving Nelson after Trafalgar. They also emphasize observation rather than intervention: recording ancient naval battles such as Actium, recovering evidence for the Punic Wars, and preserving the lost film shot at Jutland. The exchange shows how naval historians balance the desire to improve past choices against the knowledge gained from wrecks, documents, and imperfect surviving evidence.

evidence.note: The prompt and first answer cover battlecruiser design, Queen Elizabeth boilers, carriers, Nelson, and Punic War evidence.
The second answer discusses Mary Rose, Actium, and preserving the lost Jutland film.

segments.question: Which five historical events would you visit with a TARDIS?
segments.answerShort: The panel chooses moments where better design decisions or surviving evidence could transform naval history, from ancient battles to Trafalgar and Jutland.

segments.title: Four Admiral-class battlecruisers would reshape treaty fleets
segments.summary: The time-travel discussion develops a detailed counterfactual in which Britain completes Hood's three Admiral-class sisters.
segments.body: Completing four Admiral-class battlecruisers would change which older British capital ships were discarded under the Washington Naval Treaty. The panel argues that the United States might have treated each Admiral as roughly equivalent to a Colorado, possibly allowing Britain to keep them while forgoing new 16-inch ships. Britain would enter the next war with a more uniform 15-inch fleet and several fast capital ships, while repeated refits could improve the Admirals' protection and fire control. Their greater displacement might also push the treaty's individual and aggregate tonnage limits upward. Because carrier limits were linked to the same treaty system, the counterfactual could produce larger or more numerous carriers as well as a stronger battlecruiser force.

evidence.note: The panel works through retaining three additional Admirals, Washington treaty substitutions, ammunition uniformity, and likely refits.
The discussion considers their wartime use, protection limits, and the likelihood of a higher capital-ship displacement ceiling.
The counterfactual carries higher treaty tonnage into aircraft-carrier displacement and aggregate fleet capacity.

segments.title: South Dakota's Guadalcanal failure as an engineering chain
segments.summary: The panel treats South Dakota's Guadalcanal electrical failure as a systems-engineering problem worth diagramming.
segments.body: The exchange points to USS South Dakota's electrical breaker trouble at Guadalcanal as more than a simple anecdote. The panel wants to compare design diagrams with the ship as built to reconstruct the sequence of failures. That distinction matters because a cascade can expose interactions among distribution architecture, protection, construction practice, and crew actions rather than one defective component. It is a useful watch point for readers interested in how battleships could lose fighting power through internal systems failures under combat stress.

evidence.note: The prompt identifies South Dakota's cascade failure at Guadalcanal and the electrical breaker issue.
The answer proposes studying as-built diagrams and the sequence of events behind the failure.

segments.question: South Dakota had the cascade failure at Guadalcanal, right?
segments.answerShort: Yes. The panel frames it as an electrical and systems-design cascade that would reward a close engineering study.

segments.title: Sentient warships as a way to compare naval character
segments.summary: A science-fiction prompt uses famous ships, submarines, Tribal destroyers, and SS Ohio to think about ship reputations.
segments.body: The panel turns a mind-link question into a playful comparison of ship personalities. Warspite is treated as too intense, HMS Tiger and Renown as interesting possibilities, and Tribal-class destroyers as aggressive overachievers. SS Ohio broadens the discussion beyond commissioned warships by representing endurance and sacrifice in the Malta convoy story. The moment is useful because it shows how operational records and later storytelling attach distinct character to individual ships and classes.

evidence.note: The viewer asks which sentient warship the panel would share a mind link with.
The answers discuss Warspite, HMS Tiger, Renown, and Tribal destroyers as ships with distinct temperaments.
The exchange expands the definition of warship to SS Ohio and its Malta convoy reputation.

segments.question: Would you share a mind link with a sentient warship, and which one would you pick?
segments.answerShort: The panel avoids Warspite, considers several Royal Navy ships, and uses SS Ohio to show how endurance and reputation can define a ship's remembered character.

segments.title: Rodney's captain planned to fight through to the beaches
segments.summary: The sentient-warship discussion quotes an archival invasion plan in which HMS Rodney would close at night, fight at point-blank range, and beach herself if necessary.
segments.body: The panel recalls an intent statement written by Rodney's captain for the possibility of a German invasion. He proposed taking the battleship down England's east coast at night or in fog, closing so near the invasion fleet that contact might begin with a collision, and then firing every available weapon. If damage threatened to sink Rodney, the ship would beach on the invasion coast and continue fighting as a fixed gun position. The plan exploited the battleship's armor and heavy armament while accepting that survival was secondary to destroying the landing force. It is an unusually vivid example of how desperate coastal defense could overturn normal ideas about maneuver, preservation, and withdrawal.

evidence.note: The panel introduces Rodney's captain's statement of intent for responding to a German invasion.
The plan calls for close-range combat followed by beaching the damaged ship as an unsinkable gun position.

segments.title: An all-drone navy still needs defended command ships
segments.summary: A proposal for drone-centered power projection becomes a fleet-design discussion about command ships, defensive weapons, communications, and human control.
segments.body: The panel accepts that uncrewed surface and air systems can carry much of a fleet's offensive missile load, but rejects treating them as independent replacements for warships. A crewed command vessel would still need sensors, communications, electronic warfare, air defense, and close-in protection while coordinating distributed launchers. The exchange also highlights the political and legal risk of removing people from kill decisions in gray-zone encounters. It is a useful overview of why autonomy changes the distribution of functions without eliminating command, protection, or accountability.

evidence.note: The question proposes a Royal Navy centered on several types of unmanned system.
The panel separates defensive equipment on the command ship from offensive weapons on unmanned systems.
The answer warns against fully autonomous kill decisions in gray-zone encounters.

segments.question: Should the Royal Navy go all-in on drones as its main way to project power?
segments.answerShort: Drones can distribute offensive weapons, but they still require protected command, sensing, communications, logistics, and human judgment.

segments.title: Uncrewed fleets depend on contested communications
segments.summary: The drone-fleet answer turns from weapons distribution to the fragility of control links and navigation.
segments.body: A mostly uncrewed fleet would need communications that remain usable under jamming rather than merely encrypted links that work in peacetime. The panel contrasts Ukraine's fiber-optic control solutions with the difficulty of reproducing a physical connection across moving ocean forces. Narrow laser links could support local ship-to-ship control, but long-distance operations would still depend on vulnerable satellite and navigation infrastructure. Problems already seen with GPS and other satellite constellations make command over thousands of kilometers a serious design constraint. The watch point explains why autonomy cannot be evaluated separately from the network that supplies orders, position, targeting data, and human oversight.

evidence.note: The panel compares jammed radio control, fiber-optic drones, and point-to-point laser links.
The answer argues that current navigation and communications infrastructure is not ready to control a largely uncrewed force over great distance.

segments.title: Uncrewed missile craft do not remove sea-control problems
segments.summary: The answer explains why distributed missile craft still need escorts, reloads, control, and presence at sea.
segments.body: The answer treats uncrewed launchers as part of a fleet problem rather than a replacement for one. Dr. Clarke stresses that reloads, escorts, command arrangements, and control of the water around the launcher still matter. Once their missiles are spent, the craft must reach a reload point without becoming easy targets for submarines or other raiders. He also contrasts sea control with holding land: ships control the water where they are, while the rest remains contested unless mines or other presence change the situation.

evidence.note: The discussion asks how uncrewed missile craft would be escorted, reloaded, and controlled.
Dr. Clarke says the sea is not held like land and that control follows where warships are present.
The panel adds mines as another way to change control of an area.

segments.question: How would small uncrewed missile craft be escorted, rearmed, and used to control sea space?
segments.answerShort: They still need logistics, escorts, command arrangements, and sea control; uncrewed launchers do not make the ocean behave like held land.

segments.title: Wing Commander ships serve a chokepoint war
segments.summary: The panel finds the film's ships visually effective but notes that its limited battle scenes and jump-point geography shape what their designs need to do.
segments.body: The first reaction is that the Wing Commander film does not show enough of its warships to support a detailed technical judgment. Dr. Clarke nevertheless finds the designs artistically impressive while retaining questions about how well their forms would work as ships. The film's interstellar movement depends on chokepoints, so positioning and access routes matter more than unrestricted maneuver through open space. That geography helps make the story's fleet operations viable even when the hardware receives little screen time. The watch point is useful for seeing how a setting's movement rules shape fleet design and tactics.

evidence.note: The question asks for opinions on the Wing Commander film's ship designs, and several panelists initially lack enough familiarity to judge.
Dr. Clarke calls the ships artistically impressive, notes design questions, and connects the battles to chokepoint interstellar travel.

segments.question: What does the panel think of the ship designs in the film Wing Commander?
segments.answerShort: They are visually impressive but underexplored on screen, and the setting's chokepoint travel does much of the work in making its fleet operations coherent.

segments.title: Science-fiction ideas likely to appear in near-future navies
segments.summary: The panel connects science-fiction concepts with onboard manufacturing, immersive command displays, exoskeletons, and remote daughter craft.
segments.body: The answer expects ships to regain more organic repair and manufacturing capacity through advanced 3D printing, reducing dependence on contractors in every port. It then discusses circular or wraparound operations rooms adopted or marketed by Japanese and French designs as a way to improve shared situational awareness. The panel also anticipates virtual-reality views assembled from remote sensors, exoskeletons for loading and marine work, and more helicopters, drones, and intelligent expendable sensors operating from a parent ship. The larger lesson is that plausible naval innovation often arrives through sustainment, information presentation, and distributed vehicles rather than one revolutionary weapon.

evidence.note: The answer predicts sea-going additive manufacturing and contrasts it with contractor-dependent support.
The panel discusses wraparound command-center displays and their situational-awareness rationale.
The answer adds VR sensor views, loading exoskeletons, and a growing family of remote daughter vehicles.

segments.question: What science-fiction-inspired naval technologies might appear in the next few decades?
segments.answerShort: Expect improved onboard manufacturing and repair, immersive sensor displays, powered handling aids, and more remote daughter craft before more spectacular fictional technologies.

segments.title: A nuclear-cut canal would loosen carrier size limits
segments.summary: A Project Plowshare canal counterfactual connects geography, radiation risk, and the dimensions of US supercarriers.
segments.body: The proposed use of nuclear excavation would remove the Panama Canal's existing lock and beam limits, though at an enormous radiological cost. The panel notes that the ability to move ships between oceans without rounding South America has long constrained American carrier dimensions. A much wider passage could therefore encourage even larger supercarriers, either restoring air groups above one hundred aircraft or accommodating larger strike aircraft such as successors to the A-5 Vigilante. Successful nuclear excavation would also make other governments consider similarly vast canal projects. The strategic lesson is that naval architecture responds not only to combat requirements but also to the fixed geography and infrastructure through which fleets must move.

evidence.note: The question identifies Project Plowshare and the answer connects canal dimensions to carrier beam and inter-ocean movement.
The panel considers larger supercarriers, larger air groups, and heavier strike aircraft.
The discussion expands to Soviet nuclear excavation and new strategic canal routes.

segments.question: How would history change if Project Plowshare created a new canal across the Isthmus of Tehuantepec in 1960?
segments.answerShort: It would remove a major dimensional constraint on US carriers and might encourage much larger warships and further nuclear-excavated canals, at severe radiological cost.

segments.title: Carrier defense depends on mobility, stockpiles, and layers
segments.summary: A question about Patriot expenditure becomes a broader lesson in carrier mobility, missile inventories, and matching interceptors to threats.
segments.body: The panel answers that carriers are vulnerable, but not in the same way as fixed airfields or cities. A carrier group can move, withdraw when magazines run low, replenish aviation weapons, and use fighters to engage threats farther from the ships. Attacking a moving point target also demands better reconnaissance, course correction, and seekers than attacking a known land position. Even so, the answer criticizes shallow Patriot and Standard Missile procurement, because a capable interceptor is of limited use when inventories cannot sustain a campaign. The panel stresses that postwar lessons will change both offensive and defensive software, tactics, and weapons, so one conflict cannot freeze the balance permanently. It also distinguishes missile cost from the value of the target being protected while arguing for cheaper gun or short-range layers against inexpensive drones. The result is a watch point about mobility and layered defense, not a claim that carrier groups are invulnerable.

evidence.note: The answer contrasts mobile carrier groups with defended land areas and discusses Patriot and Standard Missile stockpile depth.
The panel explains lessons-learned cycles and the need to know which threats consumed the interceptors.
The discussion compares interceptor cost with protected-value and calls for cheaper close-range defenses against drones.

segments.question: Does heavy Patriot expenditure mean US carrier groups are more vulnerable to air attack than assumed?
segments.answerShort: They remain vulnerable, but mobility, fighters, seekers, replenishment, and layered defenses make the problem different from protecting fixed land targets; adequate missile inventories still matter.

segments.title: Defence ambitions have to match the money provided
segments.summary: The panel judges the announced Defence Investment Plan against the missions Britain still expects its armed forces to perform.
segments.body: At the time of the stream, Dr. Clarke says the announced four-year funding was below both the stated requirement and earlier promises. Political instability also made the plan fragile because a change of prime minister could replace it before implementation. The deeper criticism is not that Britain must choose one particular strategy, but that resources should match the missions assigned to the armed forces. A military funded for limited homeland defense cannot simultaneously provide global presence, alliance leadership, and frequent overseas operations. The exchange is a useful force-structure test: decide the desired role, cost it honestly, and either fund it or reduce the ambition.

evidence.note: The answer says the four-year figure was below the requirement and that political change could supersede the plan.
The panel argues that the government must fund the missions it assigns rather than expect global roles from a home-defense budget.

segments.question: Is there any chance the Royal Navy gets what it needs from the Defence Investment Plan?
segments.answerShort: Not from the funding described in the stream; the panel argues that Britain must either pay for its international ambitions or narrow the missions assigned to the armed forces.

segments.title: Arromanches and Le Intrepide bring real postwar ships into Azur Lane
segments.summary: A game-content question gets a concise historical appraisal of a postwar Colossus-class carrier and a Le Hardi-class destroyer.
segments.body: The panel says the event's ship choices are historically reasonable even though Arromanches was not an obvious selection. As a postwar French-operated member of the Colossus class, the carrier gives the French faction a real aviation ship without inventing a paper design. The panel had expected such a choice to arrive through a collaboration rather than a normal event, which makes its inclusion a surprise rather than a historical error. Le Intrepide receives a cooler assessment because she adds another representative of the Le Hardi class rather than a distinctly new capability. The exchange helps identify the real ships behind the game's faction and class labels.

evidence.note: The question asks about Arromanches, a battlecruiser concept, and Le Intrepide in an Azur Lane event.
The panel identifies Arromanches as a postwar Colossus-class carrier and Le Intrepide as another Le Hardi-class destroyer.

segments.question: What does the panel think of Arromanches and the Le Hardi-class destroyer Le Intrepide being added to Azur Lane?
segments.answerShort: Arromanches is a surprising but useful historical carrier choice, while Le Intrepide is a less distinctive addition because she is another Le Hardi-class destroyer.

segments.title: What the County-class destroyers lost during design
segments.summary: The panel weighs the County-class destroyers' deleted double hangar, missile magazine, forward guns, and later weapons space.
segments.body: The preferred lost feature is an earlier, longer layout with a proper two-helicopter hangar and more magazine space for Sea Slug. An alternative design removes the superfiring 4.5-inch mounting and shifts the superstructure forward to create more room aft for missiles and aviation. The discussion also explains why the ships retained substantial gun armament: designers were not certain Sea Slug would work, so proven 4.5-inch weapons provided an air-defense fallback. The design history therefore shows how technological risk can preserve older systems even when they consume space wanted for the new capability.

evidence.note: The answer identifies the deleted double hangar and larger Sea Slug magazine.
The panel proposes removing a forward gun mounting to free aft volume.
The retained guns are explained as insurance against Sea Slug development risk.

segments.question: Which deleted County-class destroyer design feature would have been most useful to retain?
segments.answerShort: The longer two-helicopter arrangement and larger missile magazine offered the clearest benefit, though a one-mount forward layout could also have freed valuable aft space.

segments.title: Battlestar fleet design through resilience and specialization
segments.summary: A Colonial Fleet redesign uses cyber resilience, logistic endurance, armored escorts, and specialized carrier battlestars.
segments.body: The first requirement is software diversity so a single compromise cannot disable the entire fleet, a lesson explicitly compared with real concern about dependence on Aegis. The panel keeps the combined battleship-carrier idea but argues for greater logistic independence and heavily armored gun-and-missile escorts. It also proposes specialist carrier battlestars that jump in, launch replacement Vipers, and withdraw, functioning like a more proactive HMS Unicorn. The result is a fleet built around resilience, replenishment, and complementary ship roles rather than identical capital ships.

evidence.note: The answer calls for software diversity and more logistically independent battlestars.
The panel proposes armored surface-combatant equivalents for closing with basestars.
Specialist carrier battlestars reinforce Viper groups and are compared with HMS Unicorn.

segments.question: How would you redesign the Colonial Fleet after the First Cylon War?
segments.answerShort: Diversify its software, improve endurance, and combine battlestars with armored escorts and specialist aviation-support carriers.

segments.title: Historical experiments with towed naval force multipliers
segments.summary: A science-fiction missile-pod question leads to real examples of towed torpedo boats, invasion barges, supply containers, and a submarine-launched V-2 concept.
segments.body: The panel notes Russian torpedo boats towed toward Tsushima and barges used to enlarge amphibious lift. Operation Sea Lion illustrates the weakness of the idea: many unpowered invasion barges would have depended on slow tugs and vulnerable towlines. Other examples include Japanese destroyers towing supply containers toward Guadalcanal and a German proposal for a U-boat to tow an upright-launching V-2 pod. The examples show that towing can add payload cheaply, but usually at a cost in speed, control, survivability, and sea-keeping.

evidence.note: The panel covers Tsushima, amphibious barges, and Operation Sea Lion's towed craft.
It adds torpedo-boat motherships and Japanese towed supply containers.
The final example is a U-boat-towed V-2 launch pod.

segments.question: Have real navies used towed systems as force multipliers like Honor Harrington's missile pods?
segments.answerShort: Yes, but examples from torpedo boats to invasion barges show that extra payload comes with severe mobility and vulnerability penalties.

segments.title: Beatty's strengths fit subordinate command better than flag rank
segments.summary: A question about David Beatty's wins becomes a discussion of aggressive command, cruiser policy, and Fleet Air Arm consequences.
segments.body: The answer gives David Beatty credit for interwar budget outcomes, County-class cruisers, and useful lower-level command roles such as gunboat and expeditionary work. His confidence and social skills might also have served alliance coordination in the Mediterranean, where diplomacy could matter as much as maneuver. The panel's sharper judgment is that those strengths were better suited to aggressive subordinate command than to high flag-rank decisions. The Fleet Air Arm example shows how Beatty's faith in political influence helped leave naval aviation under Air Ministry control, with damaging consequences for procurement and engine development.

evidence.note: The question asks for Beatty's wins and the panel cites budget and County-class cruiser outcomes.
Dr. Clarke says Beatty could be useful as a captain or commodore under someone else's strategic direction.
The answer argues Beatty was problematic in higher flag-rank decisions, especially around Fleet Air Arm control.

segments.question: What were David Beatty's wins?
segments.answerShort: He had real strengths in budget politics and aggressive subordinate command, but the answer argues those did not translate into sound high-level naval aviation policy.

segments.title: Replacing Beatty before Jutland has the widest naval effect
segments.summary: Given three historical interventions, the panel chooses replacing David Beatty before Jutland over moving Ernest King or removing L. Ron Hubbard.
segments.body: The British answer is to replace Beatty before Jutland because that would also change his staff and could remove several command and communications problems at once. The panel expects the experience of officers around the battlecruiser force to change as well, creating consequences beyond the battle itself. Moving Ernest King to the Bureau of Ordnance might improve weapons testing, but the panel treats it as a poor use of an officer whose strengths lay elsewhere. The exchange illustrates how changing a command team can have broader institutional effects than repairing one technical program.

evidence.note: The question offers replacing Beatty, moving Ernest King to ordnance, or removing L. Ron Hubbard.
Dr. Clarke chooses replacing Beatty and notes the effects on his staff and later officer experience.
The panel argues that King was better used in aviation and suggests weapon testing rather than a wholesale reassignment.

segments.question: Which intervention would you choose: replace Beatty before Jutland, put Ernest King in charge of ordnance before the Mark 14, or remove L. Ron Hubbard in 1941?
segments.answerShort: Replace Beatty before Jutland, because changing the commander and staff could alter both the battle and the later experience of the officers involved.

segments.title: Sonar is the most damaging Allied program to sabotage
segments.summary: An Axis-sabotage counterfactual identifies British ASDIC as a more concentrated point of failure than radar.
segments.body: The panel considers radar first, but rejects it because many services and countries pursued multiple radar programs in parallel. British sonar, or ASDIC, is presented as a narrower program whose disruption would more directly weaken convoy protection and the prosecution of submerged submarines. Sabotage would not guarantee an Axis victory, because Allied industrial and logistical advantages still favored a long war. It would, however, give U-boats a larger operational edge and make surface escorts much less effective against them. The exchange also stresses timing: a saboteur would have to intervene early because sonar development was already progressing through the 1920s and 1930s.

evidence.note: The question asks which Allied program to sabotage, and the panel selects sonar over the more distributed radar effort.
The answer relates Allied structural advantages and explains how losing ASDIC would weaken convoy defense.
The panel notes that an effective intervention would have to target sonar development early.

segments.question: If forced to sabotage one British or Allied program after 1930, which project would most help the Axis?
segments.answerShort: Sabotage British sonar development; unlike the many parallel radar programs, ASDIC was a concentrated capability whose loss would seriously weaken convoy escorts.

segments.title: Unryu-class carriers as a bridge to postwar Japanese designs
segments.summary: A victorious-Japan scenario explains why a proven wartime carrier might remain in production while a more ambitious successor matures.
segments.body: The panel says the Unryu class offered more room for adaptation than the question implies. A victorious Japan might keep building that cheaper, understood design while it determined what a jet-age fleet carrier actually required. The proposed G-15 successor would remain the better long-term platform, but victory would remove some of the pressure to rush it into service. The comparison with British light fleet carriers shows how an interim class can preserve numbers and experience until a more capable design is ready.

evidence.note: The question compares Unryu and G-15 carriers, and the answer treats Unryu as adaptable while G-15 remains the long-term choice.
The panel compares the approach with British light fleet carriers used until fleet-carrier designs were ready.

segments.question: Would a Japan that won the Second World War keep using Unryu-class carriers instead of moving quickly to G-15 designs for jets?
segments.answerShort: Probably as an interim measure: Unryu was cheaper and adaptable, while G-15 remained the better long-term carrier once requirements were settled.

segments.title: Destroyer captains inherited the frigate-captain tradition
segments.summary: The panel traces the aggressive culture of British destroyer command to several famous age-of-sail frigate captains rather than one model officer.
segments.body: The answer rejects the idea that British destroyer captains were simply imitators of Adam Duncan. Instead, it identifies a broader inheritance from aggressive age-of-sail frigate officers, including figures in the Cochrane and Sidney Smith mold. That tradition prized initiative, audacity, and a willingness to attack with small, fast ships. In destroyer service those qualities could be highly effective, but the panel notes that the intensity could also worry more cautious commanders. The watch point links ship type, institutional memory, and the kind of personality a navy rewards for a particular tactical role.

evidence.note: The question invokes Duncan, and Dr. Clarke instead points to Cochrane, Sidney Smith, and the aggressive frigate-captain tradition.
The answer adds that the resulting destroyer culture could make observers legitimately uneasy.

segments.question: Were British destroyers from before the First World War through the post-1945 era captained by Duncan imitators?
segments.answerShort: Not specifically; their aggressive command culture drew from a wider age-of-sail frigate tradition associated with officers such as Cochrane and Sidney Smith.

segments.title: Turning a merchant ship into a peer warship means rebuilding it
segments.summary: A Star Wars-inspired question exposes the structural and systems cost of making a civilian hull fight a purpose-built warship.
segments.body: The panel's answer is that a true peer conversion would require far more than mounting weapons on deck. Designers would have to replace or add combat computers, sensors, power and control systems, magazines, and protected hard points. The hull itself would need reinforcement so that it could carry and fire the weapons while surviving combat loads. Merchant ships are often much larger than warships, so the scale of the conversion can increase rather than reduce the bill. By the time those changes are complete, building a dedicated combatant may be the simpler and more efficient choice.

evidence.note: The answer lists computers, sensors, hard points, hull reinforcement, and the large size of merchant vessels.
The panel uses the MC80 analogy before clarifying that the question concerns a present-day civilian-to-warship conversion.

segments.question: What would it take to rebuild a civilian ship so it could challenge a contemporary warship of similar size?
segments.answerShort: So much combat-system and structural reconstruction that the project approaches building a purpose-designed warship.

segments.title: A trimaran supercarrier gains beam but loses useful volume
segments.summary: The panel explains why a very wide flight deck does not by itself make a trimaran an efficient aircraft carrier.
segments.body: A trimaran offers exceptional beam and could separate landing and launch paths across a very broad deck. The difficulty is that a carrier is not only an airfield: it also needs a large, connected hangar, power plant, magazines, workshops, and support spaces beneath that deck. Multiple narrow hulls make it harder to place the deep continuous volume aviation operations require. Only a structure large enough to bridge the hulls with a full hangar would realize the deck advantage, and the panel says current technology is not close to making that sensible. At that scale the ship might launch unusually large aircraft, but it would be an extreme engineering project rather than a practical near-term carrier.

evidence.note: The answer identifies beam as the trimaran's advantage and continuous below-deck hangar volume as its central problem.
The panel describes an enormous bridging hangar and long catapults but says the concept is beyond current viability.

segments.question: Are trimaran supercarriers a good idea?
segments.answerShort: Not with current technology; their width helps the flight deck, but the separated hulls work against the deep, continuous hangar and support volume a carrier needs.

segments.title: Why armed cargo ships do not replace dedicated warships
segments.summary: A Star Wars question distinguishes improvised armament from a hull designed for efficient frontline combat.
segments.body: The answer centers on efficiency. Ordinary transports may carry point-defense weapons or bolt-on parasite fighters, but that does not give them the systems, layout, and survivability of a purpose-built combatant. The Mon Calamari example is treated as a special case because their yards designed ships with later combat conversion in mind. The same distinction helps explain real auxiliary conversions: adding weapons is possible, but a conversion rarely reproduces every advantage of a warship designed around fighting from the keel up.

evidence.note: The panel contrasts armed transports and parasite craft with efficient frontline combatants.
Mon Calamari conversions are identified as ships built with combat modification in mind.

segments.question: If Star Wars cargo ships can be heavily armed, why build dedicated warships?
segments.answerShort: Purpose-built warships integrate combat functions more efficiently; most armed transports remain compromises unless conversion was anticipated in their original design.

segments.title: Fifteen 9.2-inch guns versus six 12-inch guns
segments.summary: A treaty-free cruiser comparison favors more 9.2-inch guns because visual-range combat rewards hit volume and rate of fire.
segments.body: For a 20,000-25,000-ton cruiser in the 1920s, the panel prefers five triple 9.2-inch turrets to three twin 12-inch turrets. The reasoning is not simply shell size: fifteen smaller guns firing at roughly twice the per-gun rate could put about thirty rounds toward a target for every six from the larger battery. At similar practical engagement ranges, that volume improves the chance of hits against cruiser targets. The exchange is a compact example of balancing individual shell power against sustained fire and hit probability.

evidence.note: The question compares three twin 12-inch turrets with five triple 9.2-inch turrets.
The answer favors the smaller guns for their much greater rate and volume of fire at similar ranges.

segments.question: Would a 1920s large cruiser be better armed with six 12-inch guns or fifteen 9.2-inch guns?
segments.answerShort: Fifteen 9.2-inch guns, because their much higher volume of fire is more valuable against cruisers at likely visual engagement ranges.

segments.title: A Wraith hive ship as an asymmetric fleet asset
segments.summary: A crossover question tests whether an unfamiliar capital ship is better used in the battle line or for deep penetration and raiding.
segments.body: The panel expects a Wraith hive ship to deliver and absorb heavy punishment if committed directly alongside Federation forces. Its greater value might come from being an unknown system that could penetrate Dominion space before the enemy understood it. The discussion considers whether Stargate ring transporters could bypass Star Trek shields to deliver explosives and whether Wraith darts could remove troops from a battlefield. Those possibilities make the ship useful for infiltration, transport, and asymmetric attack rather than only for exchanging fire with conventional capital ships. The answer remains conditional because the result depends on how technologies from the two settings interact.

evidence.note: The question places a powered Wraith hive ship in the Dominion War, and the answer weighs direct combat against a stealthy approach to the Dominion homeworld.
The panel considers shield-bypassing ring transports and Wraith darts as asymmetric capabilities.

segments.question: How would you use a Wraith hive ship under your command on the Federation side during the Dominion War?
segments.answerShort: Use its unfamiliar transport and strike systems for deep, asymmetric attacks rather than treating it only as another ship in the battle line.

segments.title: Cruiser squadrons and destroyer flotillas use different command levels
segments.summary: The answer explains why adding destroyer flotillas to a vice admiral's cruiser squadron does not automatically make each flotilla an admiral's command.
segments.body: Individual destroyer flotillas would normally remain under captains even when attached to a larger cruiser force led by a vice admiral. A cruiser squadron could already contain a rear admiral commanding its second division, especially when the formation had several cruisers. The panel says a dedicated rear admiral for light forces becomes more likely only when several destroyer flotillas are grouped together or when that officer is placed over the combined screen. The watch point shows that naval rank follows the command layer and span of responsibility, not simply the total number of hulls nearby.

evidence.note: The panel says the destroyer flotillas remain captain-led while the cruiser squadron may have a rear admiral for a division.
It adds that four or five flotillas or a combined light-forces command could justify a rear admiral.

segments.question: If a vice admiral leads a cruiser squadron, would each accompanying destroyer flotilla be commanded by a rear admiral?
segments.answerShort: No. Captains normally command the flotillas; a rear admiral appears at a higher divisional or combined light-forces level.

segments.title: Star Wars blasters lead to real particle-beam weapons
segments.summary: The panel distinguishes fictional blaster bolts from lasers and connects ion weapons with real particle accelerators and Cold War research.
segments.body: The answer describes ordinary Star Wars blasters as energy or plasma packets rather than pure laser beams, which helps explain their visible travel and interaction with lightsabers. The panel notes that different sourcebooks have given different technical language, including plasma carried or constrained by a laser. It then turns to real particle-beam weapon research pursued during the Cold War and later set aside in part because of cost and practicality. Ions can damage sensitive electronics, while medical radiotherapy demonstrates that particle accelerators already produce controlled beams for nonmilitary purposes. The missing step is a mobile weapon that can project useful effects over operational distances, not the basic ability to accelerate particles.

evidence.note: The question asks whether Star Wars weapons are lasers or plasma, and the panel describes blaster bolts as energy or plasma packets.
The answer connects fictional ion weapons with Cold War particle-beam research.
The panel discusses ion damage to electronics and particle accelerators used in radiotherapy.

segments.question: Are Star Wars weapons lasers, plasma weapons, or something else?
segments.answerShort: Blasters are usually treated as packet-like energy or plasma weapons, while real particle beams exist in research and medicine but are not yet practical mobile combat systems.

segments.title: Preserving the Chilean armored cruiser O'Higgins
segments.summary: Asked to save one pre-1900 survivor, Dr. Clarke chooses a Philip Watts-designed Chilean armored cruiser that lasted until the late 1950s.
segments.body: The panel first considers a locally remembered iron fishing vessel that survived both world wars before being sunk during the Troubles. Dr. Clarke's principal museum choice is the Chilean armored cruiser O'Higgins, launched in 1897 and completed in 1898. Built by Armstrong Whitworth at Elswick to a Philip Watts design, the ship would preserve a rare late-nineteenth-century export cruiser and its machinery, layout, and construction details. O'Higgins left service in 1933 but survived until scrapping in 1958, so preservation was historically possible. The answer highlights the museum value of ships from navies and design traditions that are otherwise poorly represented.

evidence.note: The question asks for a pre-1900 ship that survived to 1945, and the panel first mentions an old iron fishing vessel.
Dr. Clarke selects O'Higgins and gives its builder, designer, dates, and museum value.

segments.question: Which pre-1900 ship that survived until at least 1945 would you save as a museum ship?
segments.answerShort: The Chilean armored cruiser O'Higgins, a surviving Philip Watts export design that was not scrapped until 1958.

segments.title: Intercepting the Norway invasion force could change the war
segments.summary: The panel identifies an early Home Fleet interception of the German forces bound for Trondheim and Narvik as a naval battle with unusually large strategic consequences.
segments.body: Catching the northern invasion groups before their destroyers dispersed could destroy major German surface ships, troop transports, and the landing forces intended for Trondheim and Narvik. The panel argues that such a battle was operationally possible and would give Norway an unmistakable warning before the other landings arrived. Mobilized Norwegian forces could retain central and northern bases, weapons, and communications while the Royal Navy intervened around ports less exposed than Oslo. Even if Germany secured Oslo, resupply and expansion would become far harder under British submarines, mines, and surface pressure. Heavy early losses might also cause Hitler to cancel or limit the remaining assault, given his later sensitivity to capital-ship losses. A neutral or Allied Norway would divert iron ore, fish oil, and heavy water away from Germany. The answer therefore treats the Norwegian campaign as a point where one naval interception could alter geography, resources, and the later balance of the war.

evidence.note: The panel selects interception of the German Trondheim and Narvik forces and explains how it could alert and mobilize Norway.
The discussion considers British intervention, Norwegian weapon stores, and the difficulty of a German force confined near Oslo.
The panel weighs German air cover, capital-ship losses, cancellation risk, and the resource consequences of an unconquered Norway.
The answer closes with Swedish neutrality, German transit limits, and the constrained choices of Scandinavian states.

segments.question: Which Second World War naval battle, if reversed, would have the largest effect on the war?
segments.answerShort: An early Home Fleet interception of the German forces bound for Trondheim and Narvik could preserve Norwegian resistance, cripple the Kriegsmarine, and redirect strategic resources away from Germany.

segments.title: Britain could enforce blockade with contracts as well as cruisers
segments.summary: The panel explains how purchasing neutral exports could deny Germany strategic materials without seizing every cargo at sea.
segments.body: British blockade practice included buying goods before Germany could obtain them, not only stopping merchant ships with naval force. Agents purchased Swedish ball bearings during the First World War both for British use and to reduce German access. During the Second World War, Britain similarly bought most Turkish chromium production even though it consumed only a small fraction of the stock. Paying a price neutral suppliers preferred could redirect strategic materials without turning every transaction into a confrontation at sea. The watch point expands blockade from a line of warships into a coordinated system of finance, contracts, diplomacy, shipping control, and naval power.

evidence.note: The panel says British agents bought Swedish ball bearings and characterizes blockade as operating through a pen and checkbook as well as force.
The chromium example shows Britain buying Turkish production largely to deny it to Germany.

segments.title: A captured Soviet carrier would redirect British naval aviation
segments.summary: A humorous 1991 acquisition scenario becomes a serious look at how one catapult carrier could change later British aircraft and carrier choices.
segments.body: The imagined ship would first need extensive work to integrate British weapons, sensors, procedures, and support systems. Sea Harriers could use it during the transition, but a carrier built for conventional launch and recovery would encourage Britain to retain catapult aviation. The panel therefore expects stronger pressure for the F-35C and E-2 Hawkeye rather than a purely short-takeoff air group. Kept for twenty or thirty years, the Soviet-built carrier could bridge the gap to the Queen Elizabeth class and shape its design requirements. Britain might still choose gas-turbine power for the new ships, but operating a nuclear carrier would keep the alternative visible in procurement debates.

evidence.note: The question gives the Royal Navy a Soviet nuclear carrier in 1991, and the answer begins with Sea Harrier use and conversion to British systems.
The panel invents a post-Soviet purchase and treats the ship as a long-serving transition carrier.
The answer connects the catapult carrier to F-35C, Hawkeye, and later Queen Elizabeth-class choices.

segments.question: If the Royal Navy somehow acquired a Soviet nuclear carrier in 1991 and had to keep it for several decades, what would it do with the ship?
segments.answerShort: Convert it to British systems and use it as a transition carrier, likely preserving catapult aviation and influencing later aircraft and Queen Elizabeth-class decisions.

segments.title: Warhammer ships as cities with inherited crews
segments.summary: The panel explains how enormous fictional ships can contain both unused volume and overcrowded lower decks when they operate as long-lived societies.
segments.body: The answer treats a Warhammer 40,000 warship as a microcosm of its society rather than a uniformly occupied box. Entire accommodation areas may sit unused because the troops or departments intended for them are absent, while lower-deck populations crowd into the spaces they can access. Vast hull dimensions are offset by reactors, magazines, gun decks, machinery, and other systems that consume much of the interior. Crews can persist for generations, replenish their own population, absorb stranded troops, and develop distinct local cultures over centuries of service. That makes official crew figures closer to an initial establishment than a reliable count after long operation. The comparison with real ships is that habitability depends on allocation, organization, and institutional attitudes toward sailors, not simply total cubic volume.

evidence.note: The question asks why enormous ships are overcrowded, and the first answer points to unused compartments, bureaucracy, and attitudes toward crew welfare.
The panel compares the ships with cities and explains multigenerational crews and population replacement.
The discussion balances huge hull volume against machinery, weapons, informal populations, and the space needed for comfortable accommodation.

segments.question: Would the enormous ships of Warhammer 40,000 really need to be overcrowded and squalid?
segments.answerShort: Not because they lack total volume, but because machinery, unequal space allocation, bureaucracy, and centuries of self-sustaining population turn them into stratified cities.

segments.title: Why smaller torpedoes could cripple Blucher
segments.summary: The panel uses Blucher's loss to show why a torpedo-defense rating cannot be treated as ship-wide immunity.
segments.body: The question cites the Admiral Hipper-class torpedo-defense rating, but the ship under discussion is Blucher during the Norwegian campaign. The panel explains that the Whitehead torpedoes struck where the designed protective system did not provide its headline resistance. A protection figure therefore describes a defined portion and geometry of the hull, not immunity everywhere below the waterline. The answer is a useful warning that hit location can matter as much as nominal warhead weight.

evidence.note: The question contrasts the Admiral Hipper-class protection rating with the smaller Whitehead warhead that struck Blucher.
The initial answer says the hit occurred outside the area protected by the torpedo-defense system.

segments.question: How did a 120-kilogram torpedo warhead defeat a system rated against 250 kilograms?
segments.answerShort: The torpedoes struck outside the portion of Blucher's hull covered by the rated protection, so the headline resistance figure did not apply at the hit location.

segments.title: An Olympic-class hull makes an expensive cruiser
segments.summary: A fictional 1910 navy receives a supposedly unsinkable liner hull and is warned that turning it into a warship would be costly.
segments.body: The panel assigns the Olympic-class hull a cruiser role rather than treating it as a battleship. The suggested battery is built around 6-inch and 4-inch guns, appropriate to an armed cruiser or large auxiliary rather than a contemporary capital ship. Even in the fictional setting, operating such a large liner-derived hull would impose heavy costs on a small navy. Dr. Clarke therefore suggests that a conventional armored cruiser would be a more plausible and manageable acquisition. The answer shows that availability and size do not by themselves make a hull an efficient warship.

evidence.note: The question gives a fictional 1910 navy an Olympic-class hull, and the answer recommends a cruiser with 6-inch and 4-inch guns.
The panel warns about operating cost and recommends a more plausible armored cruiser instead.

segments.question: How should a fictional 1910 navy use and arm an Olympic-class hull advertised as unsinkable?
segments.answerShort: Use it as a 6-inch and 4-inch armed cruiser, but expect high costs and consider a conventional armored cruiser instead.

segments.title: A 1937 3-inch/70 would reshape Royal Navy air defense
segments.summary: The panel argues that an early rapid-fire 3-inch/70 would replace many 4-inch and pom-pom positions across the fleet.
segments.body: If the later rapid-fire 3-inch/70 had been available in 1937, the answer expects extremely broad adoption. Its rate of fire would make it preferable to many twin 4-inch installations, and even Tribal-class destroyers might trade a pom-pom position for a mount if weight could be managed. The hypothetical highlights how a mature medium-caliber dual-purpose weapon could alter prewar anti-aircraft fits across many ship types rather than merely improving one class.

evidence.note: The answer says the rapid-fire 3-inch/70 would replace many 4-inch mounts.
The panel considers a Tribal-class installation in place of a pom-pom, subject to weight.

segments.question: Which Royal Navy ships would receive a 3-inch/70 if it were available in 1937?
segments.answerShort: Almost everything that could carry it; its rate of fire would displace many 4-inch and some pom-pom installations.

segments.title: Very large missiles require a new launcher, not Mark 41
segments.summary: A question about oversized missiles explains the physical and thermal limits of the Mark 41 vertical launching system.
segments.body: The answer is categorical that a missile too large for a Mark 41 cell cannot be added by software or control-system changes alone. A new launcher must provide the necessary cell volume and handle the greater heat associated with the weapon, as seen in work on larger hypersonic missiles. Designers may reuse Mark 41 concepts or parts of its control architecture, but the ship still needs a physically different launch installation.

evidence.note: The answer says oversized missiles cannot fit Mark 41 and require a new VLS.
Control concepts may carry over, but launcher size and heat capacity must increase.

segments.question: Will missiles larger than a Mark 41 cell eventually be added to Mark 41 VLS?
segments.answerShort: No. They require a larger launcher with greater thermal capacity, even if some Mark 41 control concepts are retained.

segments.title: Science-fiction battleship design as naval systems thinking
segments.summary: A build-your-own battleship prompt mixes hull form, drives, shields, power, repair systems, automation, and layered armament.
segments.body: The panel answers a science-fiction design question by assembling ships from separate system choices rather than just naming the biggest weapon. The choices cover hull durability, faster-than-light drives, shields, power, regenerative armor, automated repair, secondary weapons, and main guns. A later answer also distinguishes internal structure from exterior protection and adds automation and mechanical loading to make the enormous hull usable. The exercise is a speculative mirror of real warship design, where survivability, propulsion, maintainability, crew requirements, and weapons must function as one system.

evidence.note: The viewer asks how the panel would design a science-fiction battleship using hulls, shields, weapons, sensors, and engines from different universes.
One answer combines a hull, advanced drives, Star Trek shields and phasers, power generation, and droid systems.
Another answer layers a 40k hull, Star Wars hyperdrive and armor, repair systems, Forerunner systems, and Wave Motion armament.
A further design combines internal structure, regenerative armor, precision drives, directed-energy weapons, and extensive automation.

segments.question: If you could design a science-fiction battleship using hulls, shields, weapons, sensors, and engines from any universe, what would you build?
segments.answerShort: The answers build composite ships around survivability, propulsion, power, self-repair, and weapons rather than treating the battleship as only a gun platform.

segments.title: Blucher's loss was a cascade, not one torpedo effect
segments.summary: A later clarification traces how gunfire, torpedoes, fire, ammunition, steam damage, and crew readiness combined aboard Blucher.
segments.body: The later discussion explains that Blucher's torpedo hits cannot be isolated from the gunfire and fires already damaging the ship. Fire reached 10.5-centimeter ammunition between machinery spaces, and the explosion violently opened additional paths for flooding and damage. A torpedo hit on a boiler room and damaged steam lines deprived turbines of power even though other boilers remained. Blucher had only recently commissioned, and German practice placed the naval crew aboard before all final trials were complete. The ship therefore entered combat with little time to build the damage-control experience needed to contain a complicated cascade.

evidence.note: The panel traces the combined torpedo, gunfire, fire, magazine, boiler-room, and turbine-room damage.
The answer notes Blucher's recent commissioning and the German practice of completing final trials with the naval crew aboard.

segments.title: How HMS Hood exceeded her original trial speed
segments.summary: The panel attributes Hood's later 33-knot performance to fuel, operating knowledge, harder running, and conservative British trial practice.
segments.body: Hood's 32.07-knot acceptance result was not an absolute ceiling. The panel points to improved fuel, greater familiarity with the ship, and a willingness to push machinery harder in later service. It also contrasts Royal Navy trials with builder-incentive trials elsewhere, where bonuses encouraged exceptionally light or aggressive runs. The watch point helps readers interpret published speed figures as products of load, procedure, incentives, and operating conditions rather than perfectly comparable constants.

evidence.note: The answer cites fuel quality, experience, and harder running.
The panel contrasts conservative Royal Navy practice with trials shaped by builder bonuses.

segments.question: How could HMS Hood later reach 33 knots after making only 32.07 knots on trials?
segments.answerShort: Better fuel, more experience with the ship, harder running, and conservative original trial practice made the later figure possible.

segments.title: Commodore rank and the US Navy's rear admiral lower half
segments.summary: The closing naval question explains why the US Navy is unusual in preferring rear admiral lower half over commodore.
segments.body: The answer reframes the question from whether navies should restore commodore to why the US Navy does not use it as a formal rank in the same way. Dr. Clarke says most other navies already have commodores and notes the American preference for rear admiral lower half and upper half. The comparison with brigadier and brigadier general shows how rank titles can reflect institutional clarity as much as naval tradition.

evidence.note: The viewer asks whether navies should reestablish commodore as a formal rank, and the answer says the main exception is the US Navy.
The answer compares commodore with rear admiral lower half and links the distinction to whether all star-rank officers are called admirals or generals.
Dr. Clarke says he does not feel strongly, but the title could make explanation easier.

segments.question: Should navies reestablish commodore as a formal rank?
segments.answerShort: Most navies already use commodore; the answer says the question is mostly about whether the US Navy should prefer commodore to rear admiral lower half.
